title: Highest temperature in London on July 24?
description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the London City Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 24 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the London City Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/gb/london/EGLC.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecast models indicate London highs of 27–29°C on July 24 under mostly settled high-pressure conditions with light winds and limited cloud cover, creating the tight spread across leading market outcomes. Minor differences in predicted afternoon solar radiation, boundary-layer mixing, and any thin high cloud or sea-breeze effects account for the close contest between 28°C (37.5%) and 29°C (27.5%). Official guidance from the Met Office and ensemble runs shows low precipitation risk but acknowledges typical 1–2°C uncertainty ranges at this lead time, consistent with historical July variability where urban heat-island amplification can push central London readings slightly above regional averages. Updated model runs and midday observations on July 23 will likely sharpen the implied probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Météo-France and ensemble guidance for Paris on July 24 point to mostly clear skies under a building ridge, supporting daytime maxima near 28–30 °C, with model spreads and slight timing differences in the thermal trough producing the tight clustering of market probabilities around 27–29 °C. Afternoon wind speeds, boundary-layer mixing, and any residual low-level moisture will determine whether readings settle at the lower or upper end of that range, while historical July variability (typical highs 24–28 °C) underscores why traders assign only modest weight to outcomes above 30 °C or below 26 °C at this lead time. Updated model runs and official bulletins through July 23 remain the key catalysts likely to shift implied probabilities.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current ensemble forecasts from the Korea Meteorological Administration point to scattered thunderstorms and high humidity on July 24 moderating Seoul’s peak temperature near 30°C, aligning with the market’s leading 37% implied probability for that exact outcome. Typical July conditions feature daytime highs of 28–31°C under the East Asian monsoon, with rain-cooled air and cloud cover often preventing stronger heating; recent short-range guidance shows light winds and 70–90% humidity further limiting diurnal rise. The 20.5% chance assigned to 32°C or higher reflects residual uncertainty in model runs that allow brief sun breaks to push readings higher, while lower bins capture cooler scenarios if heavier precipitation arrives earlier. Updated forecasts over the next 48 hours will likely tighten these ranges ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment for Tokyo’s July 24 maximum temperature centers on ensemble forecasts showing a likely peak near 32–33 °C, driven by persistent subtropical high pressure, clear to partly cloudy skies, and strong urban heat-island amplification in the Kanto plain. Recent model runs from the Japan Meteorological Agency and global ensembles indicate modest day-to-day variability tied to timing of any scattered showers or changes in boundary-layer mixing; lighter winds and higher dew points favor the upper end of the 31–34 °C range while increased cloud cover or sea-breeze effects could cap readings near 31 °C. Historical late-July climatology places average daily maxima around 31–32 °C, providing a baseline against which current model consensus is being weighed by traders.

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest ensemble forecasts from global models show Shanghai's July 24 maximum temperature centered near 35 °C, producing the tightest market clustering between 34–36 °C. Subtle differences hinge on the strength and timing of the western Pacific subtropical high, which steers warm, humid air from the East China Sea while modulating cloud cover and afternoon convection. A slightly stronger ridge would suppress thunderstorms and push readings toward 36 °C; increased moisture or sea-breeze cooling could cap the peak at 34 °C. Urban heat-island effects and boundary-layer humidity further narrow the spread, with traders weighting the latest CMA and ECMWF runs most heavily as the 48-hour window closes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Singapore's equatorial location and prevailing southwest monsoon typically produce July daily highs averaging near 31°C at official stations like Changi, with limited day-to-day variability due to consistent humidity and sea breezes. Recent NEA outlooks note maximum temperatures most often reaching 33–34°C under reduced cloud cover, while possible El Niño development favors somewhat drier, warmer conditions through the month. AccuWeather's extended July guidance shows a 28.9–32.5°C envelope, centering on 31°C. These factors concentrate trader-implied probabilities on 31–32°C, with modest upside to 33°C if afternoon convection remains suppressed; lower or higher extremes remain unlikely absent unusual steering patterns or extreme insolation. Updated model runs closer to July 24 will refine the outlook.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecast models from agencies like the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts indicate a likely daily maximum near 30–31°C in Milan on July 24, driven by typical late-July subtropical high pressure and limited advection of cooler Atlantic air. Ensemble guidance shows modest spread due to uncertainty in local boundary-layer mixing and urban heat effects, keeping the 31°C outcome at the highest market-implied probability. Recent model runs have stabilized around these values with no major shifts in the past 48 hours, though any strengthening of northerly flow or increased cloud cover could favor the 30°C bin. Traders weigh these variables against climatological baselines for the Po Valley, where peaks near 31°C align with observed trends in recent warm seasons.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from European and global models show Madrid's July 24 maximum likely peaking near 34–35 °C, producing the tightest market clustering around those outcomes. Daytime boundary-layer heating, modest southwesterly flow, and limited cloud cover favor the higher end, while any increase in low-level moisture or residual morning stratus could cap readings at 34 °C. Urban heat-island effects in central Madrid add 1–2 °C locally, yet model spread in 2-meter temperature fields remains within typical 48-hour uncertainty ranges. Updated runs over the next 24 hours from the ECMWF and AEMET will be the key catalysts likely to shift implied probabilities between these adjacent bins.

eventMetadata.context_description: **Trader sentiment clusters tightly around 28–31°C because short-range forecast models show Beijing under a weakening subtropical high with variable moisture influx from the East Asian monsoon.** Ensemble guidance from global and regional models indicates daytime maxima near 29°C on July 24, tempered by expected cloud cover and scattered showers typical of the late-July rainy period. Small differences in simulated timing and intensity of precipitation or boundary-layer mixing produce the 1–2 °C spread across leading outcomes; drier, sunnier solutions push probabilities toward 30–31 °C while stronger afternoon convection favors the lower side. Historical climatology places late-July Beijing highs near 31 °C, but current moisture anomalies and urban heat-island modulation introduce measurable uncertainty that traders price as near-even odds among the top four bins. Updated model runs and official China Meteorological Administration guidance over the next 48 hours will be the key near-term drivers of any further shifts.

This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded by NOAA at the Istanbul Airport in degrees Celsius on 24 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from NOAA, specifically the highest reading under the "Temp" column for all times on this day, available here: https://www.weather.gov/wrh/timeseries?site=LTFM

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the "Switch to Metric Units" button until the relevant table displays °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Traders assign the highest probabilities to 24–26°C as the daily maximum for Istanbul on July 24 because short-range ensemble forecasts from regional models cluster around that narrow band amid typical mid-summer variability. Istanbul’s July climate features average highs near 29°C moderated by Black Sea and Bosphorus sea breezes, but recent model runs show a transient cooler air mass and increased cloud cover suppressing peak readings by a few degrees relative to climatology. Key differentiators among the leading outcomes include the precise timing and strength of afternoon sea-breeze onset, urban heat-island amplification at official measurement sites, and any late-day convective development that could briefly elevate or cap temperatures. With only two days remaining, updated model guidance and real-time observations will continue to tighten the distribution around the current market-implied range.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast models from agencies like the Korea Meteorological Administration point to a peak daytime high near 33–34°C in Busan on July 24, driven by a strengthening subtropical ridge delivering clear morning skies and light winds before afternoon monsoon moisture triggers scattered thunderstorms. Coastal sea breezes from the East Sea typically moderate extremes by 1–2°C compared with inland areas, while high humidity and urban heat island effects in the city center can push observed maxima higher if cloud cover remains limited. Model spread on precipitation timing and wind shifts creates the tight clustering around 33–35°C outcomes, with traders weighing historical July averages near 29°C against the current ridge’s influence and potential cooling from any early rain. Updated KMA guidance and ensemble runs over the next 48 hours will likely refine these probabilities.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the King Abdulaziz International Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/sa/jeddah/OEJN.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models for coastal Jeddah project daily highs near 37–40 °C on July 24, consistent with typical July climatology of 36–39 °C moderated by Red Sea humidity and sea breezes. Recent observations show peaks reaching 40 °C, supporting the market’s elevated probabilities for 39 °C (22 %) and 40 °C (22 %). Key variables include steering winds that can suppress or enhance onshore flow, afternoon cloud cover that limits solar heating, and model spread in maximum temperature guidance, which widens uncertainty for extremes above 41 °C (33 %). Resolution hinges on official station readings, likely at King Abdulaziz International Airport, where small shifts in timing or local advection can alter the daily maximum by 1–2 °C.

eventMetadata.context_description: Traders see the highest temperature in Cape Town on July 24 clustering around 17–18 °C because short-range ensemble guidance from models such as GFS and ECMWF currently favors a typical mid-winter regime under the influence of the South Atlantic High, with light onshore flow and marine-layer stratus keeping daytime maxima near the July climatological average of 17 °C. Recent surface analyses show persistent cloud and fog along the West Coast, limiting solar heating, while any modest northerly wind shift or slight ridging could push readings to 19 °C or hold them at 16 °C. With probabilities spread across several outcomes, the market reflects genuine forecast uncertainty in boundary-layer mixing and advection just 48 hours out, and updated official guidance from the South African Weather Service will likely tighten the range before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Coastal Qingdao’s July 24 high-temperature market shows tight clustering between 30–33 °C, reflecting modest but meaningful forecast spread just 48 hours out.**  

Official guidance from the China Meteorological Administration and global models (ECMWF, GFS) currently converge on a warm, mostly cloudy day with daytime maxima near 28–31 °C, tempered by Yellow Sea breezes that limit inland-style heating. A weak short-wave trough and possible light rain or drizzle could further cap temperatures, while any delay in marine-layer formation or stronger solar heating under clearer skies would push readings into the low-30s. Historical July climatology (average daily maximum ~28 °C) places the current consensus slightly above normal, consistent with the market’s elevated probabilities for 31–33 °C outcomes. Traders are therefore pricing the narrow band of uncertainty between a typical maritime-cooling scenario and a modestly warmer, less cloudy solution, with the next model runs and any updated CMA briefing likely to narrow the distribution before resolution.

Recent model runs show limited day-to-day variability, yet the tight clustering around 34–35°C reflects uncertainty in exact timing of any convective activity and official measurement criteria at local stations. Updated guidance from regional meteorological services over the next 48 hours will likely refine these probabilities ahead of resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecasts from meteorological models point to an overcast, rainy day in São Paulo on July 24 that should cap maximum temperatures near 17–18 °C. Persistent cloud cover and showers, linked to a passing frontal system, reduce solar heating and enhance evaporative cooling at the city’s 760 m elevation, keeping surface readings below the July climatological average of 21–22 °C. Recent model runs show only modest warming potential if breaks in the clouds occur during peak afternoon hours, creating the tight clustering of market-implied odds around the two leading outcomes. Traders are weighting the latest high-resolution guidance on precipitation timing and boundary-layer moisture, which remain the key variables that could shift the daily maximum by 1 °C in either direction before official station data finalize the result.

eventMetadata.context_description: Seattle’s July 24 high hinges on the interplay between onshore marine flow from the cool Pacific, the strength of the thermal trough, and any residual marine-layer stratus that can cap daytime warming. National Weather Service and model guidance currently place the most likely range near the climatological normal of roughly 78 °F, consistent with the near-even market probabilities clustered between 76–83 °F. Small shifts in wind direction or afternoon clearing can easily add or subtract 3–5 °F, keeping the distribution broad. Ensemble spreads from the latest GFS and ECMWF runs show modest uncertainty around these values, with no strong signal for either an extreme marine push or pronounced offshore warming expected before the daily maximum is recorded.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Current forecasts from the National Weather Service and major models project a daily maximum near 83°F for New York City on July 24, 2026, under partly sunny skies with light west winds and a modest chance of afternoon showers.** This places the highest temperature most likely in the 82–83°F or 84–85°F brackets, aligning with the market’s leading implied probabilities of 47% and 26.5% respectively. 

After an early-July heat wave that produced a 100°F reading on July 2, a cooling trend has dominated mid-month, with recent days settling in the upper 70s to low 80s. Typical July climatology (average high ~84°F) is being tempered by onshore flow and limited instability, preventing significant warming while keeping readings above the cooler 80–81°F tier favored at only 17%. 

Model consensus shows low spread for this short-range period, with resolution hinging on exact cloud timing and any late-day convection that could shave a degree or two from the peak. Updated NWS point forecasts and next model runs will provide the final data points before the market settles.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent National Weather Service and private model guidance points to a high near 78–81°F in Chicago on July 24, below the 1991–2020 normal of 84.5°F, as a cooler, more stable air mass lingers with lighter winds and possible morning cloud cover limiting daytime heating. eventMetadata.context_description: Latest National Weather Service and model ensemble guidance indicate a modest high-pressure ridge supporting afternoon temperatures near 95°F in Denver, driving the tight clustering of market-implied odds around 94–97°F. At 5,280 feet elevation, rapid radiational heating under clear skies competes with variable downslope winds and boundary-layer moisture that can suppress or enhance peak readings by 2–3°F. Slight spread in GFS and ECMWF solutions for 700-hPa temperatures and wind direction creates the observed 36.5 % / 27.5 % split between the two leading bins, while lower-probability tails reflect historical July variability and potential for late-day convection. Updated model runs tonight will refine the exact maximum before tomorrow’s observation window closes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Official forecasts from the National Weather Service place Houston’s July 24 high in the mid-90s amid an ongoing heat wave driven by strong high pressure and subsidence. Model consensus shows mostly sunny skies with light southerly flow, though small differences in afternoon cloud cover, sea-breeze timing, or isolated convection create the narrow spread between the leading 94–95 °F and 92–93 °F bins. Historical July normals near 95 °F and recent observed maxima in the mid-90s anchor trader expectations, while the tight distribution reflects genuine uncertainty in short-range mesoscale details rather than any major forecast shift.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment for Los Angeles’s July 24 high centers on 80–83°F outcomes due to marine layer persistence and moderate onshore flow from the Pacific, which typically caps coastal maxima near seasonal norms of 82–84°F. Short-range models indicate variable low-cloud coverage and a weak thermal trough that may allow slight afternoon warming inland, but stronger sea breezes or deeper marine intrusion could hold readings at the lower end of that range. Historical July data show highs averaging 83–85°F under similar synoptic patterns, with deviations driven by changes in 500 mb heights or dew-point advection. Updated National Weather Service and global ensemble guidance expected within the next 12–18 hours will likely refine these narrow spreads as landfall timing and boundary-layer mixing become clearer.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current National Weather Service guidance and ensemble models indicate a moderately mixed marine layer of roughly 1,000–1,800 feet over the Bay Area on July 24, with persistent onshore westerlies and cool Pacific waters suppressing maximum temperatures near the July climatological mean of 68°F. Trader consensus favoring the 68–69°F and 70–71°F brackets reflects tight model clustering around modest afternoon warming under high pressure, tempered by uncertainty in layer depth and wind strength overnight. A shallower inversion or reduced onshore flow could allow peaks in the low 70s, while renewed stratus or stronger sea breeze would anchor readings in the mid-to-upper 60s. Final resolution hinges on official observations at the San Francisco International Airport station.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: The closely matched trader probabilities for 30–31°C as the daily maximum in Panama City on July 24 stem from typical wet-season variability, where average July highs hover near 30°C amid high humidity and frequent afternoon convection. Recent model guidance shows no significant deviations from climatological norms, with cloud cover, scattered showers, and trade-wind patterns likely capping or slightly elevating peaks depending on timing of rainfall. These factors create genuine uncertainty around the 1°C threshold, as modest differences in insolation or storm development can shift the observed high between 29–32°C. Official station data from Panama’s meteorological service will determine resolution.
